refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal scope and measurement context. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (not a one-time achievement) measured over a defined period, and that the threshold is customer-specified rather than system-imposed. This remains solution-neutral: it does not prescribe redundancy, network topology, or failover mechanisms—only the outcome requirement. The FR is atomic: it expresses a single obligation (maintain availability above threshold) without embedded 'and'/'or' conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days). Calculate availability as (total_time - downtime) / total_time. Confirm the result meets or exceeds the customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.999). Document any outages, root causes, and recovery times to support acceptance sign-off.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative pathways before availability threshold is breached.

DP3: Establish hierarchical communication architecture with local autonomous fallback mode that preserves critical teleoperation functions during link unavailability, resuming remote control upon link restoration.
DP4: Implement adaptive bandwidth and latency management with dynamic protocol selection based on measured link conditions to sustain availability within acceptable performance bounds.
DP5: Combine redundant pathways (DP1) + link quality monitoring (DP2) + autonomous fallback (DP3) as an integrated availability assurance system with coordinated state management.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Integrated availability assurance system combining (1) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, (2) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection, and (3) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback mode, coordinated through unified state management and switchover logic.

potential_sources: Coupling between failover logic (DP1) and link quality monitoring (DP2): Mitigated by clear event-driven interfaces—monitoring triggers failover decisions without tight temporal coupling.

Coupling between remote control (primary) and autonomous fallback (DP3): Mitigated by explicit mode-switching protocol and state synchronization; fallback operates independently until link restoration.
Coupling between pathway redundancy and protocol adaptation (DP4): Mitigated by treating protocol selection as a property of each pathway; each pathway can adapt independently.

Potential tight coupling in state management: Risk if unified state machine becomes monolithic; mitigation: decompose state machine into pathway-specific and mode-specific substates with clear handoff rules.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is composite but avoids tight coupling by enforcing clear separation of concerns: redundancy handles pathway diversity, monitoring handles condition assessment, fallback handles graceful degradation, and state management coordinates transitions. Each sub-DP can be designed and tested independently before integration.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target threshold (≥0.999) is technically achievable with available communication infrastructure and does not require exotic or cost-prohibitive technologies.
ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is sufficiently long (weeks to months) to capture representative failure modes and environmental variability.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits autonomous fallback mode operation; if not, DP3 must be constrained or eliminated, reducing availability assurance.

RISK: Redundant pathways may share common failure modes (e.g., both rely on same power supply, same geographic location, same ISP backbone), reducing effective redundancy. Mitigation: Require diversity analysis during DP->IM mapping.

RISK: Predictive degradation detection (DP2) requires historical baseline data; insufficient baseline may cause false positives/negatives. Mitigation: Establish baseline collection phase before evaluation period.

RISK: Autonomous fallback mode (DP3) may not preserve all teleoperation functions (e.g., high-precision manipulation); if safety-critical functions are lost, availability metric may be misleading. Mitigation: Define 'availability' explicitly—does it mean 'link up' or 'link up with full capability'?
RISK: State synchronization between remote and local systems during mode transitions can introduce race conditions or data loss. Mitigation: Implement idempotent state reconciliation and logging.

RISK: Evaluation period may not capture rare failure modes (e.g., cascading infrastructure failures); 0.999 availability requires ~8.6 hours downtime per year, which may not be observable in short evaluation windows.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to clarify: (1) Does 'link availability' mean 'communication pathway is operational' or 'teleoperation functions are available (possibly in degraded mode)'? (2) What is the definition of 'downtime'—does autonomous fallback mode count as 'available'? (3) What is the minimum evaluation period required to statistically validate 0.999 availability? (4) Are there safety-critical functions that must never fall back to autonomous mode? Revised FR: 'Maintain teleoperation link availability (defined as: remote operator can issue commands and receive feedback) at or above 0.999 over a [X-month] evaluation period, with graceful fallback to autonomous mode for non-safety-critical functions during link unavailability, and automatic resumption of remote control upon link restoration.' This revision clarifies the scope of DP5 and enables tighter IM packaging decisions.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for managing remote vehicle control, communication link integrity, failover coordination, and autonomous fallback modes during communication degradation or loss.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for monitoring redundant communication pathways, link quality metrics, and triggering failover between primary and secondary channels.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound stream of vehicle state and link quality telemetry data for real-time degradation detection and predictive analysis.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound control commands to vehicle; switches between remote operator commands and autonomous fallback directives based on link state.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety notifications when communication loss is detected or failover is triggered, ensuring Safety Services is aware of mode transitions.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Teleoperation is the natural owner of communication link management, failover logic, and autonomous fallback modes. Unified state management and switchover logic are core to remote operation continuity. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert notification requires coupling to Safety Services, but this is a necessary external dependency, not diffusion of core DP logic.

Moderate fit but suboptimal. While safety is a concern during failover, Safety Services should react to availability events, not own the availability assurance system itself. Placing DP5 here would require Safety Services to manage communication pathways, link monitoring, and switchover logic—responsibilities that belong in Teleoperation. High diffusion: redundancy logic, failover coordination, and hierarchical architecture would leak into Teleoperation anyway.

Poor fit. Vehicle module should encapsulate vehicle-side hardware and local autonomous behavior, not remote communication link management. Placing DP5 here would scatter unified state management and switchover logic across Vehicle and Teleoperation, creating tight coupling and duplicated logic. High diffusion: redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, and failover decisions are operator-side concerns.

Weak fit. NGHE Solution is likely a top-level system container, not an operational module. Placing DP5 here would be overly broad and would not clarify ownership or interface boundaries. Diffusion would be high because the actual implementation would still reside in Teleoperation and Vehicle subsystems. This boundary is too abstract to contain the concrete logic of failover and link monitoring.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 (integrated availability assurance system) is best contained within the Teleoperation module because: (1) redundant communication pathways and failover logic are inherently part of remote operation continuity; (2) real-time link quality monitoring and predictive degradation detection are operator-side responsibilities; (3) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback is coordinated from the operator station; (4) unified state management and switchover logic naturally belong in the module that orchestrates remote control. Teleoperation owns the decision to switch between remote and autonomous modes, making it the logical home for the availability assurance system.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become large if it also owns detailed vehicle-side autonomous fallback behavior. Mitigation: Clarify that Teleoperation owns switchover logic and mode selection; Vehicle owns the execution of autonomous fallback directives once mode is set.

Risk: Link quality monitoring may require deep integration with network hardware or drivers. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface abstracts hardware details; Teleoperation consumes link quality metrics, not raw network state.

Risk: Predictive degradation detection algorithms may be complex and reusable across other systems. Mitigation: Encapsulate prediction logic as a cohesive sub-component within Teleoperation; expose only link quality predictions via Telemetry interface.

Risk: Safety Services must be notified of failover events, creating a dependency. Mitigation: SafetyAlert is a one-way notification interface; Teleoperation does not depend on Safety Services for availability decisions.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable target (≥0.999 availability) over a defined evaluation period. The Teleoperation IM boundary owns the unified state management and switchover logic needed to track and maintain this metric. Link quality telemetry (inbound) and failover coordination (internal logic) provide direct observability. SafetyAlert (outbound) ensures external stakeholders are aware of mode transitions. Verification can be conducted via: (1) instrumentation of link availability metrics within Teleoperation, (2) log analysis of failover events and recovery times, (3) end-to-end testing across redundant pathways during the evaluation period.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The Teleoperation module is the unambiguous owner of availability assurance. Responsibilities are well-partitioned: Teleoperation owns redundant pathway management, link quality monitoring, predictive degradation detection, and switchover logic. Vehicle owns execution of autonomous fallback directives once mode is set by Teleoperation. Network Link interface abstracts hardware details and provides link quality metrics. This allocation prevents diffusion of core DP logic and establishes a single point of control for availability decisions.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly addresses the FR. The integrated availability assurance system (redundant pathways + real-time monitoring + hierarchical fallback + unified state management) is a coherent solution to maintaining link availability above the customer-defined threshold. The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation (e.g., automatic failover, predictive detection, autonomous fallback modes) and is testable against the acceptance criterion (measured availability ≥0.999). No logical gaps or contradictions detected between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may grow large if vehicle-side autonomous fallback behavior is not cleanly separated. Mitigation is sound—Teleoperation owns mode selection and switchover logic; Vehicle owns fallback execution. Ensure interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle clearly specifies mode directives (e.g., 'AUTONOMOUS_FALLBACK' command) rather than detailed behavior specifications.

Risk: Link quality monitoring and predictive degradation detection algorithms could be duplicated if not encapsulated as cohesive sub-components within Teleoperation. Recommend: Create internal sub-module (e.g., 'LinkQualityPredictor') within Teleoperation to centralize prediction logic and expose only predictions via Telemetry interface. This prevents algorithm diffusion across multiple modules.

Risk: Network Link interface abstracts hardware details, but if link quality metrics are not standardized, Teleoperation may need to interpret raw network state directly. Mitigation: Enforce that Network Link interface always provides normalized link quality metrics (e.g., signal strength, latency, packet loss rate) rather than raw hardware telemetry. This maintains clean separation of concerns.

Risk: SafetyAlert notification creates a one-way dependency on Safety Services. While not a diffusion risk per se, ensure SafetyAlert is fire-and-forget (asynchronous) to prevent Teleoperation from blocking on Safety Services responses. This preserves availability assurance independence.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural and optimal owner of DP5. The module boundary is well-defined: it owns unified state management, switchover logic, and coordination of redundant pathways. External interfaces are clean and unidirectional where possible (SafetyAlert, Tasking outbound; Telemetry inbound; Network Link bidirectional for monitoring). The only necessary external coupling is to Safety Services (notification) and Vehicle (mode directives), both of which are appropriate architectural dependencies. No evidence of DP logic scattered across multiple modules. Recommend formalizing the internal sub-component structure (e.g., LinkQualityPredictor, FailoverOrchestrator) to further reduce internal complexity and improve testability.

recommended_revisions: Formalize internal sub-component decomposition within Teleoperation: Create explicit sub-modules for (1) LinkQualityMonitor (real-time telemetry ingestion and metric normalization), (2) LinkQualityPredictor (predictive degradation detection algorithms), and (3) FailoverOrchestrator (redundant pathway management and switchover logic). This prevents algorithm diffusion and improves testability.

Clarify Network Link interface contract: Specify that Network Link always provides normalized link quality metrics (signal strength, latency, packet loss, estimated time-to-failure) rather than raw hardware state. This ensures Teleoperation does not need to interpret low-level network details.

Define mode directive interface between Teleoperation and Vehicle: Specify that Teleoperation sends discrete mode commands (e.g., 'REMOTE_CONTROL', 'AUTONOMOUS_FALLBACK', 'SAFE_STOP') to Vehicle, not detailed behavior specifications. Vehicle owns the execution logic for each mode.

Ensure SafetyAlert is asynchronous and non-blocking: Specify that SafetyAlert notifications are fire-and-forget (asynchronous) to prevent Teleoperation from blocking on Safety Services responses. This preserves availability assurance independence and prevents cascading failures.

Add instrumentation and observability requirements: Specify that Teleoperation must expose link availability metrics, failover event logs, and mode transition history via a monitoring interface (e.g., to a telemetry aggregator or dashboard). This enables verification against the ≥0.999 acceptance criterion.

Document evaluation period and measurement methodology: Clarify how the evaluation period is defined (e.g., continuous operation, specific test scenarios, customer-defined workload) and how link availability is measured (e.g., uptime percentage, failover recovery time, availability during degradation). This ensures acceptance criteria are verifiable and unambiguous.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is measurable (≥0.999 availability), the DP is coherent and testable, and the IM boundary (Teleoperation) is well-defined with clear ownership. However, the recommended_revisions reveal that DP5 contains multiple distinct sub-problems that warrant explicit decomposition: (1) real-time link quality monitoring and metric normalization, (2) predictive degradation detection, (3) redundant pathway orchestration and switchover logic, and (4) mode directive coordination with Vehicle. These are logically separable concerns that should be surfaced as sub-FRs to prevent internal diffusion within Teleoperation and to improve testability and maintainability. Given high safety criticality, explicit decomposition of these sub-concerns is prudent to ensure each can be independently verified and traced to requirements. Proceeding to next-level decomposition will formalize the internal sub-component structure and clarify interfaces, reducing implementation risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Monitor real-time link quality metrics (signal strength, latency, packet loss, estimated time-to-failure) from Network Link interface and normalize them into a unified quality assessment.

FR5.2: Detect predictive degradation of link quality using historical trends and anomaly detection algorithms, and alert Teleoperation control logic when degradation is imminent (before threshold breach).

FR5.3: Manage redundant communication pathways (primary and backup) and automatically orchestrate switchover to backup pathway when primary link quality falls below acceptable threshold, ensuring seamless transition with minimal latency.

FR5.4: Coordinate mode directives (REMOTE_CONTROL, AUTONOMOUS_FALLBACK, SAFE_STOP) with Vehicle based on link availability state, ensuring Vehicle executes appropriate fallback behavior when link is unavailable or degraded.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but combines four distinct sub-problems. Decomposing into sub-DPs will clarify the internal architecture of Teleoperation and ensure each sub-concern is independently addressable. This is especially important given high safety criticality: predictive degradation detection and failover orchestration must be independently testable and traceable. Recommend revising DP5 to explicitly reference sub-DPs for LinkQualityMonitor, LinkQualityPredictor, FailoverOrchestrator, and ModeDirectiveCoordinator as internal sub-components of Teleoperation.

assumptions: Network Link interface will provide normalized link quality metrics (not raw hardware state) as specified in recommended_revisions.
Vehicle module will accept discrete mode directives from Teleoperation and own execution logic for each mode (REMOTE_CONTROL, AUTONOMOUS_FALLBACK, SAFE_STOP).
SafetyAlert notifications are asynchronous and non-blocking, preventing Teleoperation from being blocked by Safety Services responses.
Evaluation period and measurement methodology for ≥0.999 availability will be formally defined (e.g., continuous operation, specific test scenarios, customer-defined workload).
Teleoperation will expose link availability metrics, failover event logs, and mode transition history via a monitoring interface for verification and observability.
open_risks: Risk: LinkQualityPredictor algorithms may require tuning or retraining based on real-world link behavior; predictive accuracy directly impacts failover timing and availability assurance. Mitigation: Establish feedback loop from failover event logs to algorithm tuning; define acceptable prediction error bounds.

Risk: Switchover latency during failover must be minimized to maintain availability; if FailoverOrchestrator introduces unacceptable delay, availability target may be missed. Mitigation: Define maximum switchover latency requirement (e.g., <100ms) and verify via instrumentation.

Risk: Mode directive interface between Teleoperation and Vehicle must be unambiguous; if Vehicle misinterprets mode commands, fallback behavior may fail. Mitigation: Formalize mode directive contract with explicit state machine and error handling.

Risk: SafetyAlert fire-and-forget pattern assumes Safety Services can handle asynchronous notifications; if Safety Services requires synchronous acknowledgment, Teleoperation may need to block. Mitigation: Clarify Safety Services interface contract early in design.

Risk: High safety criticality requires that predictive degradation detection and failover logic be formally verified or extensively tested; informal testing may miss edge cases. Mitigation: Plan for formal verification or model-based testing of FailoverOrchestrator state machine.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Network Link interface contract (normalized metrics, update frequency, error handling).
Formal specification of mode directive interface between Teleoperation and Vehicle (discrete commands, state machine, error handling).
Formal specification of SafetyAlert interface (asynchronous, fire-and-forget, payload format).
Definition of evaluation period and measurement methodology for ≥0.999 availability (e.g., continuous operation duration, test scenarios, customer workload profile).
Specification of maximum switchover latency requirement and verification method (instrumentation, log analysis, end-to-end testing).
Specification of acceptable prediction error bounds for LinkQualityPredictor and feedback loop for algorithm tuning.
Formal verification or model-based testing plan for FailoverOrchestrator state machine (given high safety criticality).
Instrumentation and observability requirements for Teleoperation (metrics exposure, log format, monitoring interface).
